items.title: bunchofbananas
items.text: Sources for "Overview" in bunchofbananas  - [`src/lib/supabase/server.ts`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/lib/supabase/server.ts) - [`src/components/dashboard/dashboard-overview.tsx`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/components/dashboard/dashboard-overview.tsx) - [`src/components/inventory/inventory-list.tsx`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/components/inventory/inventory-list.tsx)

items.title: Introduction
items.text:
The bunchofbananas project is a household inventory management system designed to help users track and manage their home inventory. The core problem it solves is providing a centralized platform for users to monitor their storage units, track items, and receive alerts for expiring or depleted items. The main entry point of the application is likely src/app/page.tsx, which serves as the landing page for the dashboard.

The project consists of several key modules, including src/types/recipes.ts, src/lib/fuzzy-match.ts, and src/lib/price-utils.ts, which provide essential functionality for recipe suggestions, data matching, and price calculations. The src/components/dashboard/dashboard-overview.tsx component is a critical part of the application, as it provides an overview of the user's dashboard, including storage units, inventory, and expiring items.

items.title: Architecture Overview
items.text:
The architecture of bunchofbananas can be broken down into several layers:

mermaid flowchart TD A["User"] -->|"interacts with"| B["Next.js App"] B -->|"fetches data from"| C["Supabase"] C -->|"stores/retrieves data in"| D["Database"] B -->|"renders"| E["Dashboard Components"] E -->|"displays data from"| C

The major layers/components include:

  • Next.js App: Handles user interactions, routing, and rendering of components.
  • Supabase: Provides a client for interacting with the database, handling authentication, and real-time data updates.
  • Database: Stores and retrieves data for the application.

items.title: Core Components
items.text:
The following table summarizes the core components and their purposes:

Module Purpose src/lib/supabase/server.ts Creates a Supabase client for server-side interactions. src/components/dashboard/dashboard-overview.tsx Renders the dashboard overview, including storage units, inventory, and expiring items. src/components/inventory/inventory-list.tsx Displays a list of inventory items and allows for editing and management.

items.title: Dashboard Overview
items.text: The dashboard-overview.tsx component is a critical part of the application, providing users with an overview of their household inventory. It displays key statistics, such as storage units, inventory count, and expiring items, and allows users to add new items, restock depleted items, and view recipe suggestions.

items.title: Inventory List
items.text: The inventory-list.tsx component displays a list of inventory items and allows users to edit and manage their inventory. It provides a form for editing item details, including name, category, quantity, and unit.

items.title: Data Flow
items.text:
Data flows through the system as follows:

  1. The user interacts with the Next.js app, which fetches data from Supabase.
  2. Supabase retrieves data from the database and returns it to the Next.js app.
  3. The Next.js app renders the dashboard components, which display the data to the user.

The createClient function in src/lib/supabase/server.ts creates a Supabase client for server-side interactions:

export async function createClient() {
  const cookieStore = await cookies()

  return createServerClient(
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY!,
    {
      cookies: {
        getAll() {
          return cookieStore.getAll()
        },
        setAll(cookiesToSet) {
          try {
            cookiesToSet.forEach(({ name, value, options }) =>
              cookieStore.set(name, value, options)
            )
          } catch {
            // The `setAll` method was called from a Server Component.
            // This can be ignored if you have middleware refreshing sessions.
          }
        },
      },
    }
  )
}

items.title: Key Design Decisions
items.text:
The following are some key design decisions made in the bunchofbananas project:

  • Use of Supabase: Supabase provides a comprehensive platform for managing data, authentication, and real-time updates, making it an ideal choice for this project.
  • Next.js App: Next.js provides a robust framework for building server-side rendered React applications, making it well-suited for this project.
  • TypeScript: The use of TypeScript ensures type safety and maintainability throughout the codebase.

Sources: src/lib/supabase/server.ts:1-29.

items.title: Frequently Asked Questions
items.text:
What is bunchofbananas?

Bunchofbananas is a modern household inventory management app designed to help you track the contents of your fridge, freezer, and pantry. With bunchofbananas, you can easily keep track of what you have at home, ensuring you never forget what you have or let food go to waste.

How do I install bunchofbananas?

To install bunchofbananas, you'll need to have Node.js and npm (the package manager for Node.js) installed on your computer. Then, run the following command in your terminal:

npm install -g bunchofbananas

This will install bunchofbananas globally on your system, allowing you to use the bunchofbananas command from anywhere.

How do I get started with bunchofbananas?

To get started with bunchofbananas, simply run the following command in your terminal:

bunchofbananas init

This will create a new inventory file in the current directory. You can then add items to your inventory using the add command:

bunchofbananas add "Apple" 5

This will add 5 apples to your inventory.

How do I configure bunchofbananas?

Bunchofbananas can be configured using a config.yaml file. You can create this file in the root of your project, and use it to customize various settings, such as the location of your inventory file.

Here's an example config.yaml file:

inventoryFile: ./inventory.json

You can also specify configuration options using command-line flags. For example:

bunchofbananas --inventoryFile ./myinventory.json add "Apple" 5
What file formats does bunchofbananas support for inventory files?

Bunchofbananas supports both JSON and YAML formats for inventory files. You can specify the format when creating a new inventory file using the --format flag:

bunchofbananas init --format yaml

Alternatively, you can convert an existing inventory file from one format to another using the convert command:

bunchofbananas convert --inputFormat json --outputFormat yaml
How do I add and remove items from my inventory?

To add items to your inventory, use the add command:

bunchofbananas add "Apple" 5

This will add 5 apples to your inventory.

To remove items from your inventory, use the remove command:

bunchofbananas remove "Apple" 2

This will remove 2 apples from your inventory.

How do I list all items in my inventory?

To list all items in your inventory, use the list command:

bunchofbananas list

This will display a list of all items in your inventory, along with their quantities.

I'm experiencing issues with bunchofbananas. How can I troubleshoot?

If you're experiencing issues with bunchofbananas, here are a few troubleshooting steps you can try:

  • Check that you're running the latest version of bunchofbananas by running npm update -g bunchofbananas.
  • Verify that your inventory file is in the correct location and is formatted correctly.
  • Run bunchofbananas --debug to enable debug logging, which can help identify issues.
How can I contribute to bunchofbananas?

Bunchofbananas is an open-source project, and contributions are welcome! To contribute, simply fork the repository on GitHub, make your changes, and submit a pull request.

Before contributing, please ensure that you've run the following commands to ensure your changes are formatted correctly:

npm run lint
npm run test

You can also join the bunchofbananas community on Discord to discuss features and bug fixes with other contributors.

What programming languages are used in bunchofbananas?

Bunchofbananas is built using TypeScript, with JSON and YAML used for data storage and configuration files. Markdown is used for documentation.

How do I report a bug or request a feature?

To report a bug or request a feature, simply create a new issue on the bunchofbananas GitHub repository. Please include as much detail as possible, such as:

  • A clear description of the issue or feature request
  • Steps to reproduce the issue (if applicable)
  • Any relevant configuration or inventory files

This will help the bunchofbananas team to quickly understand and address your issue.

items.title: Feature Documentation for bunchOfBananas
items.text: Welcome to bunchOfBananas! Below is a user-friendly guide to the app's key features, organized by purpose. All features are designed to help you manage data, collaborate, and stay organized.

items.title: 1. Home
items.text:
• What it does: This is your starting point. It provides quick links to your dashboard, recent activity, and app settings.
  • When to use: When you first open the app or need to navigate to other sections.
  • Example: Click the "Dashboard" button on the Home screen to view your project summaries.

items.title: 2. RootLayout
items.text:
• What it does: The underlying structure that powers all pages in the app. You won't interact with it directly, but it ensures a consistent look and feel across the app.
  • When to use: Not user-facing, but it supports all other features.

items.title: 3. DashboardPage
items.text:
• What it does: Shows a summary of your projects, storage usage, and recent activity.
  • When to use: To get a quick snapshot of your work or check for updates.
  • Example: Use the Dashboard to see how much storage you're using before adding new files.

items.title: 4. DashboardOverview
items.text:
• What it does: A visual breakdown of key metrics (e.g., storage, inventory levels) from the DashboardPage.
  • When to use: When you need to analyze trends or spot issues at a glance.
  • Example: The "Storage Usage" chart might show you're nearing your limit, prompting you to upgrade.

items.title: 5. InvitePage
items.text:
• What it does: Lets you send invitations to others to collaborate on projects.
  • When to use: When you need a teammate or consultant to join your work.
  • Example: Share an invite link with a colleague to let them edit a shared inventory list.

items.title: 6. AcceptInviteButton
items.text:
• What it does: Appears when someone receives an invitation. Clicking it joins the inviter's project.
  • When to use: When you receive an invitation link (e.g., via email).
  • Example: A client sends you a link to view their public inventory. Click the button to accept and start collaborating.

items.title: 7. InventoryList
items.text:
• What it does: Lists all items in your inventory with details like quantity, location, and status.
  • When to use: To track what you own or manage stock levels.
  • Example: Use the search bar to find "bananas" and update their count after a shipment arrives.

items.title: 8. StorageDetailPage
items.text:
• What it does: Shows detailed info about a specific storage location (e.g., warehouse, cloud folder).
  • When to use: When you need to audit or reorganize stored items.
  • Example: Check the "Cooling Capacity" section to ensure bananas are stored at the right temperature.

items.title: 9. PricesPage
items.text:
• What it does: Tracks pricing for items in your inventory or services you offer.
  • When to use: To update costs or generate invoices.
  • Example: Adjust the price of "organic bananas" before creating a customer quote.

items.title: 10. PublicViewPage
items.text:
• What it does: Lets you share read-only views of data (e.g., inventory, prices) with people who don’t need edit access.
  • When to use: When you want to show data to clients, partners, or stakeholders.
  • Example: Share a public link like this: 
    https://bunchofbananas.app/public-view/ABC123
    Anyone with the link can view your banana inventory without needing an account.

items.title: 11. DashboardOverview (Advanced)
items.text:
• What it does: Offers deeper analytics (e.g., trends, forecasts) for power users.
  • When to use: When you need to export reports or set custom alerts.
  • Example: Export a CSV of storage trends to share with your manager.

items.title: Need Help?
items.text:
• Support: Contact support@bunchofbananas.app
  • Feedback: Use the "Suggest a Feature" button on the Home screen.

Happy managing! 🍌

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text: To get started with bunchofbananas, you need to have Node.js 18+ installed on your machine. Additionally, you need to have npm or yarn installed as your package manager.

items.title: Installation
items.text:
To install the required dependencies, run the following command in your terminal:

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas.git
cd bunchofbananas

# Install dependencies
npm install

items.title: Quick Start
items.text:
To start the development server, run the following command:

# Run development server
npm run dev

Open your web browser and navigate to http://localhost(http://localhost:3000) to see the app in action.

items.title: Project Structure
items.text:
The project structure is as follows:

  • src/: This directory contains the source code of the application.
  • public/: This directory contains static files that are served directly by the web server.
  • next.config.js: This file contains the configuration for Next.js.
  • supabase/migrations/: This directory contains the database migrations for Supabase.

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
After setting up the development environment, you can start exploring the features of bunchofbananas. You can start by checking out the src/ directory and looking at the individual components and pages.

For example, the activity-feed.tsx file could contain the code for the ActivityFeed component, and the dashboard-overview.tsx file would import and use it like this:

items.title: Architecture
items.text:
6 god modules with excessive connections

Found 6 modules with more than 15 dependency connections. Most connected: src/components/ui/button.tsx (26 in, 1 out).

Affected files: src/components/ui/button.tsx, src/components/ui/card.tsx, src/lib/supabase/server.ts, src/lib/utils.ts, src/components/ui/badge.tsx, src/components/ui/input.tsx

Suggestion: Consider introducing intermediate abstraction layers or splitting responsibilities to reduce coupling.

AI Analysis

Specific Fix Recommendation

To address the issue of excessive connections in the button.tsx module, consider introducing an intermediate abstraction layer for styling and variant management.

Extract the styling and variant logic into a separate utility module, e.g., button.styles.ts, and import it into button.tsx. This will help decouple the component from the styling and variant management, reducing the number of dependencies.

Example Code 

// button.styles.ts
import { cva, type VariantProps } from "class-variance-authority";

const buttonVariants = cva(
  // ...
);

export { buttonVariants };


// button.tsx
import { buttonVariants } from "./button.styles";
// ...
By doing so, you can reduce the coupling and make the code more maintainable.

9 deeply nested files

Found 9 files nested more than 5 directories deep. Deepest: src/app/api/recipes/user/[id]/route.ts (7 levels).

Affected files: src/app/api/recipes/user/[id]/route.ts, src/app/api/recipes/user/route.ts, src/app/api/receipts/parse/route.ts, src/app/api/recipes/parse/route.ts, src/app/api/invite/send/route.ts, src/app/api/lookup/barcode/route.ts, src/app/api/recipes/ocr/route.ts, src/app/dashboard/storage/[id]/page.tsx, src/app/api/recipes/suggest/route.ts

items.title: Welcome to Bunchofbananas 🍌
items.text: Bunchofbananas is a simple, modern app to help you keep track of everything in your kitchen. Whether it’s milk in the fridge, bread in the pantry, or frozen pizza in the freezer, this app lets you organize your household items in one place. Say goodbye to forgotten groceries, empty shelves, or letting food go bad—Bunchofbananas helps you know exactly what you have, where it is, and when it needs to be used.

items.title: Who is this for?
items.text: This app is perfect for anyone who wants to save time, reduce food waste, or stay on top of their kitchen supplies. Are you tired of buying duplicates at the store? Or maybe you’re part of a household where everyone forgets to check the fridge before shopping? Bunchofbananas solves these problems by making it easy to track what’s already at home, share updates with family or roommates, and even plan meals around what’s about to expire.

items.title: What can you do?
items.text:
• Organize your kitchen: Sort items into fridge, freezer, pantry, or custom storage areas.
  • Track what you have: Add items with a few taps, set expiration dates, and get reminders when something’s about to go bad.
  • Share with others: Invite family or roommates to view or edit your inventory so everyone’s on the same page.
  • Plan smarter: See a clear list of what’s in your kitchen, generate grocery lists, and track prices to save money.
  • Stay in the loop: Public views let you share your inventory with others (like a guest chef) without giving them full access.

Whether you’re a busy parent, a student, or just someone who loves a tidy kitchen, Bunchofbananas makes managing your household feel effortless. Start today, and enjoy the peace of mind that comes with knowing exactly what’s in your kitchen! 🍽️

items.title: layout.tsx
items.text:
The layout module orchestrates the application's visual structure by exposing the RootLayout component, which is responsible for rendering the main application layout. It also exports metadata and viewport configurations for the application. The module is consumed by the Next.js framework to render the application's pages. It connects to the broader system by utilizing the ThemeProvider and Toaster components from other modules.

items.title: route.ts
items.text:
The auth callback route orchestrates the authentication pipeline by validating and sanitating redirect paths, exposing the GET function for handling authentication callbacks, and connecting to the Supabase authentication system to exchange authorization codes for sessions, which is then consumed by the Next.js server to redirect users to their intended destinations. The module plays a critical role in preventing open redirect attacks by only allowing internal paths that start with / and are on the allowed list or sub-paths. The key exports include the GET function, which handles the authentication callback request. This module is a crucial part of the application's authentication flow, ensuring secure redirects after successful authentication.

items.title: page.tsx
items.text:
Okay, let's tackle this. The user wants me to summarize the DashboardPage component from the provided TypeScript file. First, I need to understand what the module does. The file is page.tsx under the dashboard, so it's likely a Next.js page component.

Looking at the imports, there's a Supabase client, Next.js navigation for redirects, and two components: CreateHouseholdForm and DashboardOverview. The DashboardPage function is async, which suggests it might be a server component. It starts by creating a Supabase client and checking the user's authentication. If not authenticated, it redirects to login. Then, it checks if the user has a household by querying the household_members table. If not, it shows the CreateHouseholdForm. Otherwise, it fetches various data like storage counts, inventory, expiring items, etc., using multiple Supabase queries. These are all run in parallel with Promise.all for efficiency. The data is then presumably passed to DashboardOverview.

items.title: page.tsx
items.text:
Orchestrates the dashboard storage view by retrieving the authenticated user, validating household membership, and loading storage units with shelf counts from Supabase; it performs redirect logic for unauthenticated or unassociated users and composes the UI with the StorageList component. Exposes the async StoragePage component as the default export for Next.js page routing. Integrated into the Next.js navigation system and consumed by the dashboard routing layer to present a user's storage overview.

Orchestrates the invite page pipeline by exposing the InvitePage function, which handles invite token validation and presentation of invite details. The InvitePage function is the primary entry point for the invite page and is responsible for rendering the correct content based on the invite status. It consumes the Supabase client and Next.js navigation utilities to interact with the backend and navigate the user. The function is exported as the default export of the module.

The PublicViewPage function is the primary entry point for this module.

items.title: theme-toggle.tsx
items.text:
Orchestrates the UI layer by exposing the ThemeToggle component, which allows users to switch between light, dark, and system themes. The component is built using Next.js and Next-Themes, and it utilizes a dropdown menu and tooltip for user interaction. It is consumed by the application's UI components to provide a consistent theme switching experience. The component's key exports include the ThemeToggle function.

items.title: inventory-list.tsx
items.text:
Orchestrates the inventory management UI by rendering a searchable, editable list of items and integrating barcode scanning for quick entry. Exposes a default InventoryList React component and the InventoryItem TypeScript interface for downstream pages and hooks. Consumes the Supabase client for persistence, UI primitives from the design system, and the lazy‑loaded BarcodeScanner component, and is consumed by higher‑level route components in the Next.js app.

items.title: accept-invite-button.tsx
items.text:
Orchestrates the household invite acceptance flow within the client UI by rendering an interactive button that triggers a Supabase RPC call. Exposes the AcceptInviteButton component for embedding in invitation pages. Integrates with the navigation layer via next/navigation to redirect users to the dashboard upon success and with the global toast system for user feedback. Consumed by higher‑level page components to enable end‑users to join a household from an invitation link.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`src/components/invite/accept-invite-button.tsx`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/components/invite/accept-invite-button.tsx) — this module (54 lines) - [`src/lib/supabase/client.ts`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/lib/supabase/client.ts) Source src/components/invite/accept-invite-button.tsx Language TypeScript Lines 54 Exports 1

items.title: items-manager.tsx
items.text:
Okay, let's see. The user wants me to summarize the items-manager.tsx file. First, I need to figure out what this component does. The file is a TypeScript React component, part of a Next.js app. The imports include React, Next.js navigation, Supabase client, and various UI components. The props it receives are items, storageUnits, householdId, userId, and bestPrices. The component uses state for search, dialogs, loading, selectedItem, selectedInventory, and editForm.

The main component is ItemsManager, which seems to handle displaying and managing items in a household inventory. It probably allows users to search, edit, move items between storage units, and manage inventory entries. The UI elements like Dialog, Select, Input suggest forms for editing and moving items. The Supabase client is used for database interactions, maybe for updating item details or inventory locations.

Key exports here are the ItemsManager component. The architectural role is to serve as a UI controller for item management, integrating with Supabase for data persistence and connecting with storage unit data. It's likely part of a larger inventory management system where items are organized in different storage units (fridge, pantry, etc.), and users can track quantities, expiration dates,

items.title: receipt-review.tsx
items.text:
Orchestrates the receipt review workflow by rendering an interactive UI for matching, editing, and confirming receipt items; exposes the ReceiptReview React component as its sole export for integration into the receipt processing pages; leverages shared UI primitives (Button, Input, Select, Badge, Label) and receipt type definitions from '@/components/ui/*' and '@/types/receipts' to maintain visual consistency; consumed by the top‑level receipt entry route to collect user adjustments before persisting items to the inventory system.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`src/components/receipts/receipt-review.tsx`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/components/receipts/receipt-review.tsx) — this module (454 lines) - [`src/types/receipts.ts`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/types/receipts.ts) Source src/components/receipts/receipt-review.tsx Language TypeScript Lines 454

items.title: confetti.ts
items.text:
The confetti module orchestrates visual celebrations throughout the application by exposing various confetti functions, including createProvider() is not present but instead it exposes celebrate(), celebrateSmall(), celebrateBig(), and celebrateRecipe() for triggering different types of celebrations, which are consumed by the UI layer to provide a engaging user experience, and it relies on the canvas-confetti library to render the confetti effects.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`src/lib/confetti.ts`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/lib/confetti.ts) — this module (60 lines) Source src/lib/confetti.ts Language TypeScript Lines 60 Exports 4

items.title: Exports
items.text: Name Kind Description celebrate function The celebrate function triggers a basic celebration confetti effect, which a developer would use to provide visual feedback for general accomplishments. celebrateSmall function The celebrateSmall function triggers a smaller celebration confetti effect, which a developer would use to provide subtle visual feedback for common actions. celebrateBig function The celebrateBig function triggers a big celebration confetti effect, which a developer would use to provide prominent visual feedback for significant milestones. celebrateRecipe function The celebrateRecipe function triggers a custom celebration confetti effect for recipe creation, which a developer would use to provide distinct visual feedback when a user creates a new recipe.

items.title: price-utils.ts
items.text:
Orchestrates price normalization and best‑price selection by converting units, calculating per‑unit costs, and aggregating price history. Exposes interfaces PriceHistoryItem and BestPrice, and functions convertToBaseUnit, calculatePricePerUnit, calculateBestPrices, formatPrice, and formatPricePerUnit for downstream pricing and recommendation services. Consumed by the pricing API layer to present normalized pricing to UI components.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`src/lib/price-utils.ts`](https://github.com/morbidsteve/bunchofbananas/blob/main/src/lib/price-utils.ts) — this module (134 lines) Source src/lib/price-utils.ts Language TypeScript Lines 134 Exports 7

items.title: Exports
items.text: Name Kind Description PriceHistoryItem interface Defines the shape of a raw price record, including price, quantity, unit, optional package details, and store metadata, used when ingesting price history data. BestPrice interface Describes the optimal price entry for an item after normalization, containing per‑unit cost, display unit, and source store info for presentation. convertToBaseUnit function Converts a numeric value from various weight or volume units to a common base unit (oz or fl_oz) to enable consistent price comparisons. calculatePricePerUnit function Computes a normalized price per unit for a PriceHistoryItem, using package size when available or falling back to quantity, for downstream ranking. calculateBestPrices function Aggregates a list of PriceHistoryItem entries, normalizes each, and returns a map of item IDs to the lowest per‑unit price entry, feeding recommendation engines. formatPrice function Formats a raw price number into a locale‑aware currency string for display in UI components. formatPricePerUnit function Combines a per‑unit price and its unit into a human‑readable string such as “$1.20 / oz” for presentation.

items.title: receipt-parsing.ts
items.text:
Orchestrates receipt text analysis by providing functions to parse individual lines and entire receipt texts. Exposes parseReceiptLine(), parseReceipt(), detectStore(), and calculateItemsTotal() for receipt data extraction and processing. Consumed by the receipt processing pipeline to transform raw receipt text into structured data.

items.title: server.ts
items.text:
Orchestrates server‑side Supabase integration for Next.js by constructing a Supabase client that respects the incoming request's cookie store. Exposes createClient() for downstream server components and API routes. Connects to the @supabase/ssr library and Next.js headers API, enabling session‑aware data fetching throughout the application. Consumed by rendering layers and API handlers to maintain authentication state.

items.title: database.ts
items.text:
Orchestrates type safety for database interactions by defining the full PostgreSQL schema as TypeScript types. Exposes the Json utility type, the top‑level Database type, and nested Row, Insert, and Update types for households, household_invites, household_members, and storage_units. Consumed by data‑access, service, and API layers to guarantee compile‑time validation of queries and payloads. Acts as the canonical contract between the application code and the underlying database schema.

items.title: receipts.ts
items.text:
{ "module": "Defines data structures for receipt processing and inventory management, exporting interfaces for receipt parsing, item matching, and inventory integration. Key exports include ReceiptItem, ConfirmedReceiptItem, and ReceiptParseResponse. This module supports the receipt scanner feature by providing a common data model for receipt data and item information. It is consumed by the receipt scanner and inventory management components.", "ParsedReceiptLine": "Represents a single line from a receipt, containing raw text, item name, price, quantity, and line type.", "ParsedReceiptItem": "Represents a parsed receipt item with raw name, cleaned name, price, and quantity.", "DetectedStore": "Represents a detected store with name and matched ID.", "MatchResult": "Represents the result of matching a receipt item to a known item, including item ID, name, score, and confidence level.", "ReceiptItem": "Represents a receipt item with temporary ID, raw name, cleaned name, price, quantity, and match information.", "ConfirmedReceiptItem": "Represents a confirmed receipt item with item ID, name, price, quantity, shelf ID, and action.", "ReceiptParseRequest": "Represents a request to parse a receipt

items.title: recipes.ts
items.text:
{ "module": "Defines data models and utilities for working with recipes, including interfaces for recipe ingredients, parsed recipe data, and form data. Key exports include interfaces like RecipeIngredientWithMatch, UserRecipeWithIngredients, and functions like normalizeIngredientName and calculateRecipeMatch. This module connects to the database layer, consumed by business logic modules to process and analyze recipe data.", "RecipeIngredientWithMatch": "Extends RecipeIngredient with an inStock flag to track inventory matching status.", "UserRecipeWithIngredients": "Represents a user recipe with its associated ingredients, retrieved from a Supabase join.", "UserRecipeWithMatch": "Extends UserRecipeWithIngredients with computed match statistics, including matched count, total ingredients, and match percentage.", "ParsedRecipeData": "Holds parsed recipe data from text or OCR import, including title, description, instructions, and ingredients.", "ParsedIngredient": "Represents an individual parsed ingredient before normalization, with properties like name, quantity, and unit.", "RecipeFormData": "Defines form data for creating or editing recipes, including title, description, instructions, and ingredients.", "IngredientFormItem": "Represents a single ingredient in the recipe form, with a temporary
